edition.name: Music Concept Knowledge Bank — Ultimate Codex Edition
edition.purpose: Authoritative, level-exclusive, machine-readable knowledge bank for generating text-only Millionaire questions about musical concepts normally assessed through listening.

scope_policy.level_exclusivity: The assessed target concept and correct answer must belong to the selected qualification level. Lower-level vocabulary may appear only as necessary supporting language or carefully chosen distractors; it must not become the knowledge being tested.
scope_policy.text_only_requirement: Questions generated from this bank must function without audio.
scope_policy.aural_relevance_requirement: Although questions are text-only, they must focus on features pupils could identify, infer or understand from listening: sound, musical function, instrumentation, texture, form, rhythm, melody, harmony, tempo, performance technique or notation that represents those audible features.
scope_policy.historical_trivia_policy: Do not generate questions whose answer depends primarily on dates, years, composer names, biographies or chronology. Such information is retained only in background_only and source_audit.
scope_policy.fact_source_policy: Generate questions only from question_eligible_facts, aural_profile, senses, distinguish_from, comparison groups and resolved Music Literacy links. Do not generate from background_only or unapproved source extracts.
scope_policy.difficulty_policy: Difficulty is intentionally unassigned. The teacher will tag concept and question difficulty later. Legacy intrinsic-complexity values are retained only for audit and must not drive question selection.
scope_policy.multiple_meanings_policy: When a term has more than one musical meaning, the question must identify the intended context and, where available, reference the correct sense_id.
scope_policy.odd_one_out_policy: Odd-one-out and classification questions must state the criterion explicitly, such as 'Which is not an ornament?'. Do not ask a generic unexplained 'odd one out' question.
scope_policy.ambiguity_policy: Every multiple-choice question must have exactly one defensible correct answer. Rewrite or discard questions where more than one option could be correct.
scope_policy.audio_wording_policy: Do not write 'listen to', 'in the excerpt' or similar wording unless an audio asset is actually provided. Use written musical scenarios instead.

id_policy.concept_id_rule: Permanent sequential identifier assigned to the 300 concepts in this final edition. New concepts must use the next unused number; existing IDs must never be renumbered.
id_policy.canonical_key_rule: Human-readable lookup key. Use concept_id as the permanent foreign key.

concept_types.historical_or_compositional_style: A named style or period identified by a recurring musical language.
concept_types.musical_style: A named musical style identified by characteristic sound, rhythm, instrumentation, harmony, texture or production.
concept_types.vocal_genre: A vocal work, song type or dramatic vocal category.
concept_types.instrumental_genre: A type of instrumental composition or performing tradition.
concept_types.dance: A dance type identified through metre, tempo and characteristic rhythm.
concept_types.pitch_direction: The upward or downward direction of pitch.
concept_types.melodic_movement: Movement between neighbouring or non-neighbouring pitches.
concept_types.melodic_device: A process used to create, repeat, answer or develop melodic material.
concept_types.harmonic_object: A simultaneous pitch relationship or harmonic sonority.
concept_types.harmonic_change: A change of chord, key or harmonic state.
concept_types.chord_progression: A level-specific sequence or set of functional chords.
concept_types.chord_type: A chord identified by its interval content or harmonic function.
concept_types.cadence: A two-chord phrase ending identified by harmonic movement and aural effect.
concept_types.scale_or_tonality: A pitch collection or system of tonal organisation.
concept_types.ornament: A decorative melodic note or figure.
concept_types.pitch_effect: A performed change or slide in pitch.
concept_types.pulse_or_metre: The organisation, grouping and subdivision of the beat.
concept_types.tempo_marking: A term indicating relative musical speed.
concept_types.tempo_change_or_instruction: A term describing a change, flexibility or restoration of speed.
concept_types.rhythmic_device: A recognisable pattern, displacement or transformation of rhythm.
concept_types.performance_role: The role of a featured line relative to accompaniment.
concept_types.texture_or_imitative_device: The relationship between simultaneous musical parts, including imitation.
concept_types.accompaniment_or_sustained_pattern: A repeated, sustained or patterned supporting part.
concept_types.musical_form: The large-scale ordering and return of musical sections.
concept_types.structural_section: A named section or passage with a particular function within a larger form.
concept_types.fugue_component: A named thematic role or process within a fugue.
concept_types.fugue_or_sonata_subject: A principal theme whose precise meaning depends on fugue or sonata context.
concept_types.serial_or_transformational_technique: A method of ordering or transforming musical material.
concept_types.instrument_family: A family of instruments grouped by sound-production method.
concept_types.ensemble: A named group of performers or instruments.
concept_types.ensemble_role: A subgroup or role within a larger ensemble.
concept_types.vocal_technique: A way of producing, organising or using the singing or speaking voice.
concept_types.articulation: How notes are connected, separated or attacked.
concept_types.instrumental_technique: A physical method or written instruction that changes how an instrument sounds.
concept_types.electronic_or_timbre_effect: An effect that alters tone colour, pitch or perceived acoustic space.
concept_types.sound_source: A broad source of musical sound.
concept_types.accompaniment_pattern: A patterned arrangement of chord tones used as accompaniment.

tag_namespaces.associated-family: Instrument family associated with a style, ensemble or technique.
tag_namespaces.aural-domain: Primary audible dimension used for identification.
tag_namespaces.classification: Useful explicit class for odd-one-out questions.

allowed_question_families: odd one out with an explicit criterion
notation-linked identification after resolving Hub IDs
prohibited_question_families: composer names, biographies or chronology as the sole tested fact
physical trivia with no clear connection to sound or identification
questions that require an unheard audio extract
ambiguous wording where a term has more than one musical meaning
a correct answer whose assessed level does not match the selected qualification level
distractor_policy: Prefer concepts in the same comparison group or concept type.
Make distractors increasingly close and plausible as question difficulty increases.
The correct answer must occur exactly once.
Do not use an alternative sense of the same term as an unmarked distractor.
hint_policy: Use aural features, classification tags and distinctions as the source.
Guide strongly without including the correct concept name.
Do not copy the full definition.
Riddles, common sayings, word associations and concise musical clues are permitted.

question_record_reference_contract.conditional_foreign_key: sense_id when the concept has multiple meanings
question_record_reference_contract.separation_rule: Keep generated questions in a separate question-bank file; do not embed them in this knowledge bank.

hub_integration.music_literacy_link_strategy: Each concept may contain lookup terms and a relationship type. Codex must inspect the existing Music Literacy data and code, resolve the exact existing IDs, and populate target_literacy_ids without creating duplicate concepts.
resolution_order: manual teacher review where no unique match exists

concepts.definition: An African American style that developed from spirituals, work songs and field hollers. It is commonly in 4/4 and often follows a 12-bar chord pattern, although 8-bar and 16-bar forms also occur. Characteristic features include blue notes, call and response, repeated riffs and improvisation.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Blues started as Black American folk music, developing from spirituals and work songs. Blues music is often in 4/4 time and is mostly patterned on a 12-bar structure (although 8 and 16 bars are also found) and on a scale where some notes are flattened.
Due to its origins in slavery, blues music tends to have a sadness to it.
source_extracts.status: Cleaned source extract retained for audit only; use the approved definition and fact_statements for question generation.

revision_history.reason: Combined repeated references to 12-bar form and flattened scale notes while retaining the main stylistic facts.

Characteristic features include blue notes, call and response, repeated riffs and improvisation.

concepts.definition: An African American style that emerged in the early 20th century from traditions including blues and ragtime. Characteristic features include syncopation, improvisation, distinctive harmony and instrumental solos, performed by ensembles ranging from small groups to big bands. Common instruments include saxophone, trumpet, piano, bass and drum kit.

concepts.definition: Dance music associated with Latin America, often featuring lively syncopated or off-beat rhythms, a strong dance pulse and prominent percussion. Course examples may use instruments such as bongos, güiro, castanets or claves.

concepts.definition: A staged dramatic work that combines spoken dialogue, songs, acting and usually dance. The performers are accompanied by a band or orchestra, and the songs help to develop the story or characters. Unlike most opera, a musical may include substantial spoken dialogue and commonly uses musical-theatre or popular-style vocal delivery, although some modern works are sung throughout.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition: A series of related chords built on notes of a major or minor scale. The chords all share notes from the same scale, and it is this property that allows the formation of a series of chords that will sound cohesive as opposed to somewhat random. == National 4 == At National 4 level chords are built on the first, fourth and fifth notes of a major or minor scale. Listen to this song, which uses chords I, IV, and V. Notice that the actual order of chords is I, IV, I, V. 'Lean On Me' by Bill Withers == National 5 == At National 5 level chords are built on the first, fourth, fifth and sixth notes of a major or minor scale. Listen to this song, which uses chords I, IV, V and VI. Notice that the actual order of chords is I, VI, IV, V - this is a chord progression associated especially with 50s and 60s pop music. 'Stand By Me' by Ben E King Chords I, IV, V and VI have been used in a vast number of songs. The Australian comedy band 'Axis of Awesome' have taken those chords and shown how many different songs fit the progression I, V, VI, IV. Axis of Awesome - '4 Chords'

concepts.definition: The steady beat is organised into recurring bars containing two, three or four main beats. The first beat of each bar is normally the strongest and helps the listener sense the metre. In simple time the common signatures are 2/4, 3/4 and 4/4; in compound time, 6/8, 9/8 and 12/8 represent two, three and four main beats respectively.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A bar containing either 2, 3 or 4 beats.
When we listen to music, the beat or pulse will normally form groups of two, three or four.
In simple time we use the time signatures 2/4, 3/4 and 4/4 to display whether the music has 2, 3 or 4 beats in the bar. 4/4 can also be written as C, which stands for 'common time'.
In compound time we use the time signatures 6/8, 9/8 and 12/8 to display whether the music has 2, 3 or 4 beats in the bar.

fact_statements: The regular underlying pulse of the music - the steady beat that a listener might tap or clap along with.
Tempo describes how quickly this pulse moves.
Beats are commonly grouped in twos, threes or fours, with the first beat carrying the main stress.

concepts.definition: Music with a strong, regular walking pulse, normally organised in two or four beats per bar. Marches are designed to support coordinated movement and often use clear accents and percussion. Scottish marches are often performed by pipe bands, solo pipers, fiddle groups or Scottish dance bands.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Music with a strong steady pulse, with two or four beats in a bar, and at a speed that is suitable to march to.
A piece of music originally dating back to Egyptian times when music was used as part of the advance of an army and which is still used for military purposes. In Scottish music, military marches are usually played by pipe bands (a band of bagpipes and drums) but also by solo instruments, military bands, Scottish dance bands, fiddle orchestras and modern-style Scottish folk and Celtic rock groups.
Marches are sometimes combined into a 'set' of 'March, Strathspey and Reel'.

concepts.definition: Notes or accents occur between, or on the weaker parts of, the main beats rather than directly on the strongest pulse. Off-beat patterns can create lift, surprise or syncopated energy. A common 4/4 example places rhythmic emphasis on beats 2 and 4 rather than the stronger beats 1 and 3.

distinguish_from.difference: On-the-beat notes reinforce the main pulse; off-the-beat notes occur on weaker parts or between the main beats.
Off-beat placement is one source of syncopation; syncopation more broadly displaces expected accents through off-beats, ties or emphasis.

concepts.definition: A lively Scottish dance in simple time, usually with two or four beats per bar. Reels have a quick, flowing pulse, are commonly in a major key and are often played by fiddle, accordion or a Scottish dance band. Each main beat divides evenly into two or four.

A reel is fast and flowing; a strathspey is slower and characterised by dotted rhythms and Scotch snaps.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A musical instrument having a flexible bag inflated either by a tube with valves or by bellows, a double-reed melody pipe called the chanter, and from one to four drone pipes.
Bagpipes can play solo music known as pibroch, or join with other pipers and drums to form a pipe band. Bagpipe competitions are common in Scotland, and both solo pipers and pipe bands can compete for numerous prizes and awards.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
There are four main sections to an orchestra: strings, woodwind, brass and percussion:
Strings include 1st and 2nd violins, violas, cellos and double basses.
Woodwind includes flutes (including piccolo), oboes, clarinets, bassoons.
Brass includes French horns, trumpets, trombones and tubas.
Percussion instruments include timpani (kettle drums) and other instruments which are hit or struck, e.g. snare drum, bass drum and xylophone.
Other instruments, such as the harp, are sometimes included in the orchestra.

concepts.definition: A keyboard instrument that produces sustained tones from pipes or electronic sound generators. Pipe organs are strongly associated with churches, and several notes can be held while the keys remain pressed. Large pipe organs commonly have more than one keyboard, called manuals, plus a pedalboard played with the feet.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Instruments that are played by hitting, striking, shaking or scraping.
Percussion instruments can be divided into two groups; tuned percussion and unturned percussion.
Tuned percussion can play a range of notes at different pitches, for example the glockenspiel, timpani or xylophone.
Untuned percussion mostly only have one pitch or sound, e.g. bongo drums, cymbals, drum kit, güiro, snare drum or triangle.

concepts.definition: A keyboard instrument in which pressing a key causes a felt-covered hammer to strike a string. It can play melody and harmony together and has a wide pitch and dynamic range. Its full name, pianoforte, reflects the ability to play softly or loudly through changes in touch. Here the term means the instrument, not the Italian dynamic marking piano, which means soft.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Instruments that are played by hitting, striking, shaking or scraping.
Percussion instruments can be divided into two groups; tuned percussion and unturned percussion.
Tuned percussion can play a range of notes at different pitches, for example the glockenspiel, timpani or xylophone.
Untuned percussion mostly only have one pitch or sound, e.g. bongo drums, cymbals, drum kit, güiro, snare drum or triangle.

concepts.definition: A broad course label for music drawn from African traditions. Common classroom identifiers include layered percussion, repeated rhythmic patterns, call and response, singing, clapping and overlapping rhythms; these features vary greatly between regions and cultures. Interlocking rhythms and improvisation may be added to the layered percussion and call-and-response textures used in course examples.

concepts.definition: Music from approximately 1600-1750. Common features include basso continuo, contrapuntal texture, ornaments, sequences, terraced dynamics and instruments such as harpsichord and organ. The harpsichord and basso continuo were central to much Baroque ensemble music, and J. S. Bach and George Frideric Handel are major composers associated with the period.

source_audit.source_status: cross-checked and selectively enriched from supplied course materials, the Musipedia XML export and My Music Online

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Reggae music was developed in the late 1960s in Jamaica.
The bass guitar often plays the dominant role in reggae. The bass sound in reggae is thick and heavy, and equalized so the upper frequencies are removed and the lower frequencies emphasized. It is common for reggae to be sung in Jamaican English.
Reggae is noted for its tradition of social criticism and religion in its lyrics, although many reggae songs discuss lighter, more personal subjects, such as love and socializing.
One of the most easily recognizable elements is off beat rhythms; staccato chords played by a guitar or piano (or both) on the off-beats of the bar.

concepts.definition: Music from approximately 1810-1900, following the Classical period. Common features include expressive melody, rubato, chromatic harmony, wide dynamic range, programmatic ideas and an expanded orchestra. The orchestra expanded to include instruments such as piccolo and trombone and a wider range of percussion; Berlioz, Chopin and Liszt are representative composers.

concepts.concept: Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V in major keys
listed_as: Chord progressions - chords I, IV and V (major keys)
concepts.definition: A sequence built from the tonic, subdominant and dominant triads of a major key. Chord I is built on the first scale degree, IV on the fourth and V on the fifth; in C major these chords are C major, F major and G major. All three are normally major triads and can form many common progressions and cadences.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
The music sounds in a minor key (tonality), often described as having a dull, dark, evil or sadder feel than major.
The minor scale is built on an order of tones and semitones and follows the musical alphabet (e.g. A B C D E F G A). The order is tone, semitone, tone, tone, semitone, tone, tone. This is the natural minor scale which sounds modal.
In order to make the scale sound better the last interval is reduced to a semitone. This makes the previous interval a tone and a half (e.g. A B C D E F G# A). This is the harmonic minor scale.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
The notes which appear before the first strong beat of a musical phrase, particularly at the start of a piece. An anacrusis sounds as an upbeat.
The well-known song 'Happy Birthday to You' has an anacrusis. The word 'Happy' appears before the first beat of the bar (making this the anacrusis), and the word 'birthday' lands on the bar line. The well-known song 'Away in a Manger' has an anacrusis. The first syllable of 'Away' appears before the first beat of the bar (making this the anacrusis), and the second syllable lands on the bar line.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
The music has two, three or four beats in each bar, and each beat is a crotchet (1 beat note) and each beat can be divided into 2. If you try saying the word 'sim-ple' in each beat, and it fits with the notes, then you are listening to simple time.
Simple time signatures are written as 2/4, 3/4 and 4/4. 4/4 can also be written as C, which stands for 'common time'.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A Scottish dance with four beats in a bar, with dotted rhythms, and usually featuring the Scotch snap.
A Strathspey often comes before a reel. It is in simple time and is usually in a major key, at a slow tempo.

fact_statements: A Scottish dance in simple quadruple time, usually at a moderate tempo.
It is identified by dotted rhythms and especially the Scotch snap.
In a traditional dance set, a strathspey commonly precedes a faster reel.

A passage of music which allows soloists to display their technical ability in singing or playing an instrument.

concepts.definition: A strict type of imitation in which one part begins a melody and another enters later with the same melody. The parts continue together, so the melody overlaps with itself. Because the following parts reproduce the melody exactly, a canon is stricter than general imitation.

distinguish_from.difference: Imitation may be altered; canon requires a strict delayed copy of the melody.
A round is a cyclic canon in which each part repeats from the beginning; a canon need not loop continuously.
Stretto overlaps entries of a fugue subject before the previous entry finishes; canon is a strict imitation of a complete melody.

common_misconceptions: Canon and general imitation are always interchangeable terms.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition: This instrument belongs to the string family. It is usually about 1.8 metres high, weighs 36kg and has 47 strings. Each string corresponds to a white key on a keyboard. There are 7 pedals on a harp that correspond to the 7 musical 'pitches', i.e. one pedal for 'A', one pedal for 'B', etc. Each pedal has a middle 'natural' position, as well as 'sharp' and 'flat' positions. Every 'C' is coloured red and every 'F' is coloured dark blue/black.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A keyboard instrument which looks like a small grand piano. The keys are laid out in the same way, as on a piano but are opposite in colour – the majority of the keys are black, and the raised ones are white. When a key is pressed, it causes a string to be plucked, which gives the harpsichord a 'twangy' sound.
The harpsichord was popular in the 15th and 16th centuries, which was around the time of the Renaissance and Baroque periods.

concepts.definition: An untuned drum with metal wires stretched beneath the lower head, producing a crisp rattling sound. It is played with sticks or brushes and is common in orchestras, bands and drum kits. A lever can engage or release the metal snares, changing between a rattling snare sound and a drier side-drum sound.

source_audit.source_status: cross-checked and selectively enriched from supplied course materials, the Musipedia XML export and My Music Online

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
This instrument belongs to the percussion family and is played with sticks or brushes. It can act as two different instruments – a side drum and a snare drum.
The snare drum has a set of curled, metal wires stretched across the bottom skin of the drum. These are called the snares.
Using the lever on the side, the snares can either be right up against the bottom skin of the drum – which makes it a snare drum and has a 'rattly' sound – or moved away from the bottom skin – which makes it a side drum with a 'dry' sound where no rattles can be heard.

concepts.definition: A large group of singers, or a section written for that group, within a larger work such as an opera or oratorio. A chorus normally sings in several parts and may comment on or participate in the action. The word can also mean the recurring refrain between verses of a song; in this record, the assessed sense is the choral group or the music written for it.

concepts.definition: Music of the Classical period, approximately 1750-1810. concepts.definition: A Scottish Gaelic work song traditionally sung by women while rhythmically beating newly woven cloth to soften and shrink it. source_extracts.cleaned_definition: Atonal music has no feeling of key, major or minor. It is very dissonant, and it will lack a 'nice' melody and accompaniment. Atonal music is a feature of some 20th-century music.

fact_statements: Music with no established tonal centre and no functional major or minor key.
All pitches may be treated with similar importance, so the harmony often sounds unsettled or dissonant.
Atonality became an important feature of some early-20th-century music.

concepts.concept: Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key
listed_as: Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key
concepts.definition: A sequence using four common triads of a major key: I is the tonic major chord, IV the subdominant major chord, V the dominant major chord and VI the submediant minor chord. Their order creates familiar patterns used in many songs. Together, these four chords can produce the principal perfect, imperfect, plagal and interrupted cadence patterns.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Term used to describe the effect of two notes being played against three (eg in piano music it might be groups of two quavers in the right hand and groups of triplets in the left).
The term is also used to describe the effect that occurs when the accents in a piece of music are different from those suggested by the time signature (eg the division of 4/4 time into 3+3+2 quavers).

fact_statements: Contrasting rhythmic groupings or patterns occur simultaneously, so the parts imply different accent patterns.
This can create the effect of competing metres without necessarily changing the written time signature.

concepts.definition: Expressive flexibility of tempo in which the performer slightly speeds up and slows down while preserving the broader flow of the music. Unlike a concert harp, it has no pedals; levers or blades may alter the pitch of individual strings.

concepts.definition: The persistence and reflection of sound after it is produced. Electronic reverb can make a recording seem as though it was performed in a larger or more resonant space. It consists of dense, rapidly repeated reflections that merge into a resonant tail rather than a clearly separated echo.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
An electronic effect which can give the impression of different hall acoustics. For example, reverb can make a piece of music sound as if the performance is taking place in a cathedral.
Reverb is the name given to the natural series of very short and dense reflections of a sound that occur in a confined space such as a room or a hall. While echoes with a longer delay would be discernible, in reverb the echoes happen so fast and are so dense it is impossible for the listener to hear individual repeats. Reverb is the essence of natural sound.
Listening to a close mic'd instrument is like having the instrument play in your ear in a very small room. The addition of reverb to a sound makes it appear as if the instrument is being played in a real acoustic environment, for example a church or concert hall.
Nowadays reverb can be emulated digitally very easily and nearly all effects processors have a wide range of reverb types for different applications

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Originally these forms were performed in homes and mainly by amateur musicians – as you can imagine, space was limited, therefore this music was written for a small instrumental ensemble with one player to a part.
Popular chamber music groups include, the Piano Trio (Piano, Violin, Cello), String Quartet (Violin 1, Violin 2, Viola, Cello) or Octet – an ensemble with 8 players (often wind, strings or a combination of both). Over time, these forms have migrated into a concert hall setting.

concepts.definition: A late-19th- and early-20th-century style that emphasises atmosphere, suggestion and tone colour rather than strongly directed tonal drama. Common features include whole-tone or modal scales, unresolved or extended harmony, parallel chords and delicate orchestration. Piano writing may use the sustain pedal, tied notes and cross-rhythms to blur pulse and sonority.

source_audit.source_status: cross-checked and selectively enriched from supplied course materials, the Musipedia XML export and My Music Online

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A number of Arts movements develop as a response to what had come before. Impressionism was no different, being developed as reaction to the chromatic nature of music from the Romantic period.
Music written in the impressionist style mirrors the style of painting by Artists such as Claude Monet, where edges between objects are often blurred. This effect was created in the music through the use of cross rhythms, often tying notes across a barline, and on piano, particular use was made of the sustain pedal.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition: Jazz funk is a sub-genre of jazz music. Many of the key features found in jazz music are evident here too – a strong rhythmic ‘groove,’ above which instrumentalists improvise solo passages. The most significant difference from jazz are the instruments within the ensemble. These will typically consist of drum kit, bass guitar, rhythm guitar and synthesiser, in short then, it is the merging of traditional Jazz characteristics with electronic instruments requiring electronic amplification. It is a style that emerged during the 1970s with the ensemble combinations and driving groove patterns being similar with disco.

source_audit.provenance: H & AH Listening Dictionary - normalised definition; cross-checked against Musipedia page(s): Lied; cross-checked against My Music Online level indexes and selected definition pages
source_audit.source_status: cross-checked and selectively enriched from supplied course materials, the Musipedia XML export and My Music Online

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
This term (the German word for song) refers to songs for solo voice, accompanied by piano. This is a style which emerged during the Romantic Period and was pioneered by Schubert who wrote over 600 songs in this style. Other composers include Beethoven, Schumann and Liszt.
The text is in German, and they can be either strophic (verse/chorus structure) or through-composed (no sections of the song are repeated).
An interesting feature of Lieder is that the voice and piano are equally important – both the voice and the piano having opportunities to be showcased and where the writing for each is intertwined.
'An Die Musik' by Schubert is an example of a Lied is in Strophic form (i.e. the same music is used for each verse). It is sung by a Baritone.
'Erlkönig' (again by Schubert) is an example of a Lied which is through-composed (there is no repeat of sections). This song is a story involving different characters (narrator, father, son and Erlkönig). At different times in the performance, the singer changes his body language and tone of voice to portray the different characters.

concepts.definition: A musical setting of the fixed sections of the Roman Catholic Mass. Major movements commonly include Kyrie, Gloria, Credo, Sanctus, Benedictus and Agnus Dei, and settings may range from a cappella Renaissance polyphony to large choral-orchestral works. Traditional settings use Latin text, and performance forces range from unaccompanied choir to soloists, chorus and orchestra.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
The Mass is a sacred choral work traditionally using the five main sections of the Roman Catholic, Western Orthodox, Anglican or Lutheran Church liturgy.
Features of the Mass include Latin text and polyphonic texture. The first masses composed during the Rennaissance period would have been sung A cappella.
During the Baroque period composers like JS Bach extended the form by composing masses scored for both chorus and one or more solo voices with orchestral accompaniment which naturally led to works being of a larger scale.
In the Classical period, composers like Haydn and Mozart continued to develop the genre with solo voices taking on a more prominent role within compositions. The first example is Haydn's 'Kyrie' from 'Mass in Bb Major' - the writing for the group of soloists is cleverly matched against the chorus.
The opening of Mozart’s Mass in C Minor, 'Kyrie' is very heavily scored for both Orchestra and largely homophonic writing for the chorus paving the way for a much lighter texture when the Soprano soloist begins.
In the 20th Century British composer Benjamin Britten wrote 'Missa Brevis in D' for a choir of treble voices accompanied by Pipe Organ. In this example listen to how Britten skilfully blends and resolves discords.

fact_statements: A musical setting of the fixed sections of the Roman Catholic Mass.
Major movements commonly include Kyrie, Gloria, Credo, Sanctus, Benedictus and Agnus Dei, and settings may range from a cappella Renaissance polyphony to large choral-orchestral works.
Traditional settings use Latin text, and performance forces range from unaccompanied choir to soloists, chorus and orchestra.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition: Some of the very earliest examples of pieces of music include Plainchant. Also known as Gregorian Chant or Plainsong, Plainchant was the mainstay of music in the early church. Songs have Latin text and are sung both unaccompanied and in unison. Plainchant has no regular metre (set beats in a bar) the chant follows the natural rhythm of the text with many of the individual syllables having their own musical note. Originally Plainchant would have been sung by monks as part of worship.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Sonata can be exemplified in two ways.
A composition for solo instrument (usually piano), or
a composition for a solo instrument accompanied by piano.
In the classical period a sonata would normally contain 3 movements – a fast movement, a slow movement and a final fast movement. As the style developed through the classical and romantic periods, sonatas often became longer and composers began to add in additional movements.

source_audit.source_status: cross-checked and selectively enriched from supplied course materials, the Musipedia XML export and My Music Online

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Soul music developed in the southern states of America and grew in popularity throughout the 1960s. It was a combination of gospel, blues and country music, and its gritty sound reflected what was happening socially in America at that time.
One of the main centres for soul music was in Memphis Tennessee, home of STAX records, where musicians from different ethnic backgrounds joined together to write and record music. At this time, racial segregation was still very much part and parcel of everyday life for Americans and was certainly a barrier to creative collaborations such as writing and recording music. Artists recording with STAX included, Aretha Franklin, Otis Redding, Sam & Dave, Isaac Hayes, Booker T & the MG’s, Staple Singers to name a few.
In Detroit, Michigan, a style of soul music was also developing. Although it had the same heartfelt emotion and passion in the music, the sound was smoother than their counterparts at STAX. Detroit was the home of Motown Records. Artists who recorded with Motown included the Jackson 5, Diana Ross, Martha Reeves, Marvin Gaye and Smokey Robinson.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition: This describes a note 'added' to the familiar Chord structure (root, 3rd and 5th). So for the Chord of C which includes the root (C), 3rd (E) and 5th (G) notes, the addition of the 6th note (A) creates the Chord C6. This chord is used frequently in jazz and popular music.

fact_statements: A major or minor triad with the note a sixth above the root added.
The extra note gives the chord a warm, mellow colour strongly associated with jazz, swing and popular harmony.
For example, adding A to the notes C-E-G produces the chord C6.

concepts.definition: A four-note chord built from stacked minor thirds. Its symmetrical construction produces strong tension and tonal ambiguity, allowing it to resolve in several directions and making it useful for dramatic effects. Its symmetry makes it especially useful for modulation to distant keys.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A diminished 7th chord consists of four notes built one on top of the other. It is built up using intervals of a minor 3rd - a minor 3rd describes two notes which are 4 semitones apart e.g. C-Eb. The interval between the lower and top note is an interval of a diminished 7th, giving the chord its name. This chord is most often used when modulating to distant keys.
This is a diminished 7th chord built on C. Try it for yourself and hear how it sounds.

concepts.definition: A minor scale with the seventh degree raised by a semitone in both ascending and descending forms. Its interval pattern is tone-semitone-tone-tone-semitone-tone-and-a-half-semitone.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A scale is a series of notes. The harmonic minor scale is formed using the following pattern: tone, semitone, tone, tone, semitone, tone and a half, semitone.
This is how the scale of C harmonic minor looks when written down:
A harmonic minor scale can begin on any note. Here is the scale of G harmonic minor:
A harmonic minor scale shares the same key signature as its relative major but raises the 7th note by a semitone, both ascending and descending.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A cadence is formed by two chords at the end of a phrase.
An interrupted cadence is usually formed by the chords V–VI. (In the key of C major, chords G to A minor.) This creates an ending which can sound unfinished – where the listener is surprised that the music has drawn to a close.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition: The distance in pitch between two notes, e.g. 'C' - 'F' is a 4th. The interval is counted from the lower pitch to the upper pitch inclusive, i.e. 'C' (1), 'D' (2), 'E' (3), 'F' (4). It is known as a 4th as it is the 4th note from 'C' (remember to count your starting note as 1).

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A scale is a series of notes. The melodic minor scale is formed using the following patterns:
tone, semitone, tone, tone, tone, tone, semitone (when ascending)
tone, semitone, tone, tone, semitone, tone, tone (when descending)
This is how the scale of C melodic minor looks when written down:
A melodic minor scale can begin on any note. Here is the scale of G melodic minor:
A melodic minor scale shares the same key signature as its relative major but raises the 6th and 7th note by a semitone ascending, and returns them back down a semitone descending.

concepts.definition: Music based on a mode rather than the familiar major or minor scales. Each mode has its own pattern of tones and semitones and may create a distinctive tonal colour. The Dorian mode, for example, can be demonstrated by playing the white notes from D to D; its pattern is tone-semitone-tone-tone-tone-semitone-tone and it is widely used as a basis for jazz improvisation.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Term used to describe music based on a mode. There are a number of different modal patterns which were later refined and developed into our modern major and minor scale structures. Like a scale, modes can be built on any note.
One of the most common modes is the Dorian Mode. It is formed using the intervals tone, semitone, tone, tone, tone, semitone, tone (e.g. D E F G A B C D). Following this pattern, you can begin the mode on any note. Here are the notes for the Dorian mode beginning on C:
Modes are used in many jazz and pop music pieces where they provide an excellent underlying harmonic basis for improvisation.
There are seven different modes. For more information on modes and how to build them please watch the video example.

concepts.definition: A short ornament in which the main note moves rapidly to the adjacent note above or below and returns. The upper and lower forms are distinguished by their symbols and direction. An upper mordent uses main note-upper neighbour-main note; a lower or inverted mordent uses main note-lower neighbour-main note.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Each major key has a relative minor which shares the same key signature. If we picked a major key, such as C Major for example, to find its relative minor key we would move down three semitones, which would take us to A Minor.
The opposite is also true, if we were in a minor key and wanted to modulate (change key) to the relative major, we would move up three semitones. For example, if we were in E minor, an upwards movement of three semitones would take us to G Major.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
In simple time music is easily grouped into two quavers whilst in compound time the groupings are of three quavers.
So in 4/4, the music naturally groups into 4 crotchet beats, whilst in 6/8, the grouping would tend to fall into a pattern of 2 groups of 3 quavers (or 2 dotted crotchets).
Irregular time signatures occur when the music does not naturally fall into equal groupings.
5/4 where there are 5 crotchet beats in each bar is perhaps the most common of irregular time signatures. It is not possible to divide the grouping of notes equally within the bar. This results in an irregular grouping pattern of 3 and 2, or 2 and 3.
Listen particularly to the opening of 'Take 5' which is in 5/4 time. The music is grouped like the first example above – 123 + 12. This is especially clear in the first 20 seconds or so before the saxophone soloist enters.
In 'Money' the music is in 7/4. The grouping of the beats in this example is 1234 + 123.

fact_statements: Metres with an unusual number or grouping of beats, such as 5/4, 5/8, 7/8 or 7/4.
Their beats are often grouped unevenly, for example 2+2+3, creating an asymmetrical pulse.
A 5/4 bar commonly groups its beats as 3+2 or 2+3.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Sometimes referred to as continuo. In the Baroque period, the continuo part consisted of a bass line (basso continuo) which could be played by cello, bass, viola da gamba or bassoon depending on which instruments were available. Layered on top of this, the harpsichord, organ or lute player would fill in harmonies built on that bass line. This would provide the foundation upon which the melody sits.
Sometimes figures were written under the bass line, this was called Figured Bass. These figures would indicate how the player should build the chord above the bass line note to create the harmony. Bach is one Baroque composer who used this extensively in his work.

concepts.definition: A Baroque concerto that contrasts a small group of soloists, the concertino, with the larger ensemble, the ripieno. source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A concerto grosso is an important form of music which developed throughout the Baroque period. It has 3 main elements:
Concertino - describes the small group of soloists
Ripieno - describes the larger ensemble group
Basso continuo - describes the bass line and harmony.
In a concerto grosso, the small group of soloists (concertino) are combined and contrasted with the ensemble (ripieno).

source_extracts.cleaned_definition: An aria (solo song) in ternary form (ABA) first appeared in the works of Handel and Bach during the Baroque period. It was further developed in the 17th and 18th centuries. Da capo arias are found within the scores for oratorio and opera. Typically in the score of a da capo aria, the third section is not written out but the instruction da capo (from the beginning) is given instead. The repeat of the A section is performed with the solo ornamented.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Ritornello is used to describe a theme which returns frequently throughout a piece of music, or a movement within a larger piece. It is often associated with Rondo form - where the key theme keeps returning interspersed with different material.
Ritornello is a key feature of the Concerto grosso, where the Ripieno (ensemble) group play this recurring theme between different sections played by the Concertino.

concepts.definition: A large-scale form with three main stages: an exposition presenting contrasting subjects, a development that transforms and explores them, and a recapitulation that returns the subjects in the tonic key. source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Sometimes known as first movement form (because it is the structure commonly used for the first movement of a symphony). This term is used to describe the structure of the first movement of many sonatas, symphonies and often overtures. It falls into three sections:
Exposition – where two key contrasting themes are introduced. These are in related keys
Development – the section where these key themes are developed
Recapitulation – where the key themes are heard again, this time in the same key

paraphrased_source_facts: The page identifies exposition, development and recapitulation and the tonal relationship of the subjects.

fact_statements: A large-scale form with three main stages: an exposition presenting contrasting subjects, a development that transforms and explores them, and a recapitulation that returns the subjects in the tonic key.
A coda may follow.
It is also called first-movement form because it frequently structures the opening movements of sonatas and symphonies.

concepts.definition: A form in which substantially new music continues from beginning to end instead of the same music returning for each verse. Earlier ideas may recur, but there is no regular strophic repetition. This form is especially suitable for narrative texts because the music can change continuously with the words and dramatic situation.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A vocal/choral composition in which there is little or no repetition in the musical structure i.e. where the composition is not structured as verse/chorus. Some of the best examples of through-composed songs include those from Lied. 'Erlkönig' is through-composed. Listen to the way the singer changes his body language and tone of voice to portray the different characters (narrator, father, son and Erlkönig).
The structure of through-composed songs is not confined to the romantic period, listen to 'Bohemian Rhapsody' by Queen.

concepts.definition: Highly agile, ornamented vocal writing involving rapid scales, arpeggios, leaps, trills and other decorations. It is especially associated with high soprano roles and displays the singer's range and technical control. The style became prominent in Baroque vocal music and may include both written and improvised ornamentation.

concepts.definition: High, light tones produced by touching a string lightly at a nodal point while bowing or plucking. This suppresses the fundamental and allows an upper partial to sound clearly. On bowed strings they can sound high and eerie; on guitar and harp they often have a bell-like quality.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Ripieno is one of the elements of the Baroque concerto grosso. It describes the larger / main group of ensemble instruments.
The other concerto grosso elements include the:
Concertino – describes the small group of soloists; and
Basso continuo – describes the bass line and harmony.
In a concerto grosso, the small group of soloists (concertino) are combined and contrasted with the ensemble (ripieno).

Term for the rapid up-and-down movement of a bow on a stringed instrument creating an agitated, restless effect.
Tremolando also describes rapid alternation of two different notes at least a 3rd apart played on piano, strings, wind, brass and tuned percussion instruments.

concepts.definition: An English sacred choral work using a religious text, originating in the Renaissance and associated with Protestant worship. It may be a cappella or accompanied by organ or orchestra and can include solo passages, contrasting sections and homophonic or polyphonic writing; it is the English counterpart of the Latin motet.

concepts.definition: A broad term for recent and present-day jazz styles. It combines improvisation with features such as modal, extended or highly chromatic harmony; complex, changing or cross-rhythms; grooves built from a small number of chords; electronics; extended techniques; and influences from funk, rock, world music, film music, free jazz or the avant-garde.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Contemporary jazz is an umbrella term for all kinds of jazz music being played now - as well as jazz music of the 80s, 90s, 00s & 10s – which can feature some or all of the following:
sophisticated
highly chromatic harmonies (verging on impressionist or atonal)
rhythmic experimentation (cross rhythms, changing time signatures)
development of a groove based on just two or three chords
instruments used in experimental ways (melodic instruments used in percussive roles, harmonics and other virtuoso performing techniques)
world music and avant garde influences and inclusion of instruments never used seriously in jazz before (flugelhorn, flute or oboe)
Further info including reviews and biographies can be found on the All Music website.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Electronic dance music is normally heard in clubs where the DJ combines tracks electronically into one smooth mix. Electronic dance music originally featured drum machines, synthesisers and sequencers but is currently now mostly produced using computers and software that contains sampling, effects, and multitrack recording features.
Electronic music can encompass music of different genres including house music, dubstep, drum and bass.

concepts.definition: A secular Renaissance vocal work, usually for several unaccompanied voices. It is commonly polyphonic and through-composed, with changes of texture, rhythm and harmony used to reflect the meaning of the words. In the English madrigal tradition, English texts, word painting and a cappella performance are common features.

concepts.definition: A sacred Renaissance polyphonic choral work, usually in Latin, using a religious text other than the fixed movements of the Mass. concepts.definition: A 20th-century style, prominent from the 1920s, that revives the balance, clarity, forms and textures of Classical or earlier music while using modern harmony, rhythm and orchestration. It often favours smaller ensembles and clear formal design as a reaction against late-Romantic excess; Stravinsky and Prokofiev are associated composers.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
New classicism. From about 1929 onwards this style in music came about when composers reacted against Romanticism and wanted to return to the structures and styles of earlier periods but combined with dissonant, tonal and even atonal harmonies.
The composers started to write for smaller orchestras. Stravinsky and Prokofiev were two of the composers of this style.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Renaissance means 'rebirth' and denotes a period in history where there was a resurgence of interest in culture and learning based on the ideas of the ancient Greeks and Romans.
It was a period of great progress in science and astronomy while great explorers from European nations ventured over the oceans on great voyages of discovery. Sacred choral music still remained as the most important music but composers started to take a much greater interest in composing secular music - music for use out with the church.

concepts.definition: Music organised by a predetermined series rather than traditional functional tonality. In twelve-tone serialism, a tone row orders all twelve chromatic pitch classes and is transformed through techniques such as transposition, inversion and retrograde. Arnold Schoenberg developed twelve-tone serialism in the early 20th century.

concepts.definition: An expressive non-chord note that takes a significant part of the following main note's value. It normally creates accented dissonance, is approached by leap and resolves by step into a chord note. In a common traditional interpretation, it takes half the following note's value, or two-thirds when that note is dotted.

concepts.definition: The minor triad built on the second degree of a major scale. In first inversion its third is the bass note; this form is often labelled IIb or ii6 and commonly leads towards chord V as a predominant harmony. In C major, chord II is D-F-A; its first inversion places F in the bass, giving F-A-D.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
In a major key, chord number two tends to be a minor chord. To explain, let's look at the scale below:
Chord II in a major key is a minor chord. This is because the note which tells us if a chord is a major or minor (the 3rd) in this case, is a minor 3rd.
Look at the scale of C major. We already know that chords 1,4 and 5 are major chords because they contain major 3rds. Chord 6 is a minor because it contains a minor 3rd.
When we play the above chord with the D as the lowest note, we say that it is a D minor chord in root position.
If we moved the position of the chord so that the F was the note at the bottom of the chord, and the other two notes (in this case A & D) were on top, we would say that this is a D minor chord in 1st inversion.
1st inversion is when the chord is positioned so that the 3rd of the chord is the lowest note.

concepts.concept: Chords I, IV, V and VI in major and minor keys
listed_as: Chords I, IV, V and VI in major and minor keys
concepts.definition: Triads built on the first, fourth, fifth and sixth scale degrees. In major, I, IV and V are major while VI is minor; in harmonic minor, i and iv are minor, V is major because of the raised leading note, and VI is major. These chords can form all four principal cadence patterns: V-I perfect, any progression to V imperfect, IV-I plagal and V-VI interrupted.

Chords I, IV, V and VI in major and minor keys

source_extracts.page_title: Chords I, IV, V and VI
source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Chords I, IV, V and VI are important as, between them, we can form any of the four cadences.
In a major key, it is normal for chords I, IV and V to be major chords. Chord VI is normally a minor chord. In a minor key, it is normal for chords I and IV to be minor chords. Chords V and VI are normally major chords.
The easiest way to remember this is to think of the table below:
So, if the key were F major, then chord I would be F major, chord IV would be Bb major (same), chord V would be C major (always major), chord VI would be D minor (opposite).
And if the key were D minor, then chord I would be D minor, chord IV would be G minor (same), chord V would be A major (always major), chord VI would be Bb major (opposite).

concepts.definition: An ordered arrangement of all twelve chromatic pitch classes, normally using each once before any is repeated. It provides the basic material for twelve-tone serial music and can be transposed, inverted, reversed or combined with these transformations. Because all twelve pitch classes are used before repetition, no tonic or dominant is automatically privileged.

fact_statements: An ordered arrangement of all twelve chromatic pitch classes, normally using each once before any is repeated.
It provides the basic material for twelve-tone serial music and can be transposed, inverted, reversed or combined with these transformations.
Because all twelve pitch classes are used before repetition, no tonic or dominant is automatically privileged.

concepts.definition: A contrapuntal work built from a subject introduced successively by several independent voices. In the exposition, the first voice states the subject, the next normally gives an answer at the dominant while the first continues with a countersubject, and further voices enter in turn. Later subject entries alternate with episodes and may use devices such as sequence, inversion or stretto.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition: A contrapuntal piece based on a theme (subject) announced in one voice part alone, then imitated by other voices in close succession. Listen to the excerpt from the Fugue in C Major for Organ by J S Bach. The subject is quite distinctive and can be clearly heard entering, each time lower than the entry before. In a fugue, the second time the melody enters it is called the answer as it enters at a different pitch, usually the dominant of the original key, for example the first note of the subject would be C and the first note of the answer would be G. As the answer plays, the first part continues with the countersubject above. The subject then appears again, as the original part enters free fugue. The table below helps show this structure.

concepts.definition: A transformation that reverses the direction of each melodic interval, so an upward interval becomes the corresponding downward interval and vice versa. The term can also describe chord inversion, where a note other than the root appears in the bass. In chord inversion, first inversion places the third in the bass and second inversion places the fifth in the bass.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
Like most music of the Rennaissance period if not actually a dance, like a pavan or galliard, was certainly dance like in style.
The music could be played by solo instruments such a lutes, harpsichords or virginals, by small groups of instruments of the same family or a group of varied instruments from different families. In England, a consort was the name given to a group of instruments of the same family playing together.
It was also a common practice to perform music with a 'broken consort', a collection of different instruments which blended together. This was really the forerunner of the Baroque orchestra which developed during the 17th century.

source_extracts.cleaned_definition:
A piano trio is a chamber music ensemble comprising of three instruments; the most common form comprises of a piano, violin and cello.
The form, which developed throughout the classical period, initially had a 3 movement structure. A fast movement, a slow movement and a final fast movement. This is the same overall structure as both a sonata and a concerto of that period.
Other combinations of instruments can be found, but this is less common that the typical piano, violin and cello grouping.

source_history.previous_document_purpose: Master, level-exclusive definition bank for generating no-audio Music Concept questions across National 3, National 4, National 5, Higher and Advanced Higher.

previous_scope_policy.level_exclusivity: The assessed target concept and correct answer must belong to the selected qualification level. Lower-level vocabulary may appear only as necessary supporting language, not as the knowledge being tested.
previous_scope_policy.audio_requirement: All records are suitable for text-only question construction. Questions must not depend on hearing an excerpt.
previous_scope_policy.difficulty_model: Question difficulty is separate from intrinsic concept complexity.

previous_enhancement_policy.level_boundaries: Original qualification-level ownership is retained. Musipedia level labels and related links do not reassign concepts.
previous_enhancement_policy.definition_method: The existing normalised definition remains the core. Curated supplementary details from both completed review passes were merged, semantically deduplicated and limited to accurate, relevant, curriculum-useful information.
previous_enhancement_policy.question_bank_safety: Supplementary details are phrased so they can support text-only recall, comparison and application questions without requiring audio.
previous_enhancement_policy.source_audit: Cleaned Musipedia source extracts and page revision metadata are retained in each matched JSON record for auditability.
previous_enhancement_policy.my_music_online_method: The A-Z index, the qualification-level index pages and selected individual definitions were compared against the existing three-source bank. Useful facts were paraphrased, duplicated wording was removed, and audio, quizzes and non-essential examples were excluded.
previous_enhancement_policy.conflict_resolution: Where website tables or individual pages differed from one another or from the supplied curriculum documents, the project concept list and supplied reference materials remained authoritative. No level or element was reassigned from website data.
previous_enhancement_policy.definition_quality: The third-source pass also removed repeated wording from earlier source merges while retaining all useful assessed facts.
previous_enhancement_policy.scope_authority: The user-supplied concept list and reference documents remain authoritative for qualification level and musical element. No level or element was reassigned from website data.
previous_enhancement_policy.question_bank_readiness: Definitions preserve enough distinct factual statements for easy recall, medium comparison and hard application questions without importing audio-dependent information.

mymusic_online_index_confirmed_source_ga.status: The current Higher concept page confirms the concept and supplied a useful tritone relationship; the project definition has been retained and strengthened without changing level ownership.

previous_my_music_online_review.editorial_outcome: My Music Online substantially corroborated the existing reference-booklet and Musipedia-enhanced bank. The final pass adds useful examples or distinctions where available, resolves combined-label ambiguities and removes repetition introduced by earlier source merges.
